sources.title: CONSORT 2025 explanation and elaboration: updated guideline for reporting randomised trials
sources.locator: Eligibility and recruitment; item 17 and boxes 5-6 (sequence generation, simple/restricted/stratified randomisation, minimisation); item 18 (concealment and implementation); item 20 (blinding and end-of-trial guesses); box 1 and intervention/comparator guidance (effect definition and attention controls)

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: Used only to verify current reporting guidance on eligibility, sequence generation, simple and restricted randomisation, stratification, minimisation, concealment through the allocation event, party-specific blinding, limitations of end-of-trial allocation guesses, effect definition, and comparator description. The lesson's timeline, examples, questions, assessment, explanations, and wording are independently created; no sentence, example, figure, table, or checklist is reproduced or adapted. Although the displayed licence is CC BY 4.0, the use remains facts-only original expression.

agentAccess.notes: Before opening the article, the finalizer checked the current PMC robots route and NCBI policies. PMC explicitly allows /articles/ for all user agents with a one-second crawl delay; NCBI publishes rate and identification conditions and no source-specific prohibition on this open article route. Access used the public PMC HTML page without login, paywall, rate-limit, or access-control bypass. This is a dated observation, not a publisher warranty.

sources.title: Blinding in Clinical Trials: Seeing the Big Picture
sources.locator: Definition of blinding and its distinction from allocation concealment; the parties who may be blinded; blinding where interventions cannot be masked; reported magnitudes of exaggeration by non-blinded outcome assessors

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: Used only to verify which parties can be blinded, that blinding is graded rather than all-or-nothing, the separation of blinding from allocation concealment by timing, the availability of blinded outcome assessment when participants cannot be blinded, and the reported average exaggeration associated with non-blinded outcome assessors. Although this source is CC BY 4.0 and expressive reuse would be permitted, no wording, example, figure, or table is reused; the lesson states these facts in independently written prose and its own instructional structure.

agentAccess.notes: Before opening any article, the accountable contributor read https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/robots.txt and https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/robots.txt, plus the NCBI website policies at https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/home/about/policies/. The PMC robots file explicitly allows the /articles/ path used here for all user agents with a one-second crawl delay, and the policies page states rate and identification conditions for automated access without prohibiting agent reading of open-access articles. Access was a single request to the public article URL with no login, paywall, rate-limit, or access-control bypass. This is a dated observation of the source's terms, not a warranty from the publisher.

sources.title: Association Between Risk-of-Bias Assessments and Results of Randomized Trials in Cochrane Reviews: The ROBES Meta-Epidemiologic Study
sources.locator: Results: ratios of odds ratios for random sequence generation, allocation concealment, and blinding; between-trial heterogeneity for subjective outcomes

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: Used only to verify the study scale, exact ratios of odds ratios and credible intervals, the definition of those ratios on an oriented odds-ratio scale, and the reported heterogeneity result. The final lesson independently explains why the ratios are not fixed changes in patient benefit or absolute outcome differences. No wording, figure, table, or dataset is reproduced or adapted; although the source is CC BY 4.0, the use is facts-only original expression.

agentAccess.notes: The finalizer rechecked the PMC robots route and NCBI policies before a single public HTML request. PMC allows /articles/ for all user agents with a one-second crawl delay; access used no login, paywall, rate-limit, or access-control bypass. This is a dated observation, not a publisher warranty.

sources.title: Impact of blinding on estimated treatment effects in randomised clinical trials: meta-epidemiological study
sources.locator: Abstract and results: 142 meta-analyses and 1,153 trials; party- and outcome-specific ratios of odds ratios and credible intervals; discussion and conclusion on no detected average difference, imprecision, residual confounding, and retention of blinding as a safeguard

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: Used only to verify the study scale, party- and outcome-specific ratios of odds ratios, wide credible intervals, absence of a detected average difference, and the authors' uncertainty about residual confounding, imprecision, and interpretation. The lesson's synthesis, examples, assessment, and wording are independently created. The displayed NonCommercial licence is outside the project's expressive-reuse allowlist, so no wording, figure, table, or dataset is reproduced or adapted.

agentAccess.notes: Before opening the article, the finalizer checked the current PMC robots route and NCBI policies. PMC explicitly allows /articles/ for all user agents with a one-second crawl delay; NCBI publishes rate and identification conditions and no source-specific prohibition on this open article route. Access used the public PMC HTML page without login, paywall, rate-limit, or access-control bypass. This is a dated observation, not a publisher warranty.

sources.title: Sampling: how to select participants in my research study?
sources.locator: Target population, sample and sample frame; sample representativeness and sampling bias; probability and non-probability sampling methods; effect of non-response

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: Used only to verify the distinction between target population, sample frame, and sample; that convenience and other non-probability routes are generally not generalizable to the target population; and that non-response related to the object of research can distort an estimate. The lesson's own cases and wording are independent, and no example, table, or sentence is reproduced or closely paraphrased. The source licence is NonCommercial and therefore outside this project's permitted expressive-reuse bases; it is used for facts only.
